lesson.title: Joint Distributions
lesson.body: The joint PMF p(x,y) = P(X=x, Y=y) for discrete random variables satisfies Σₓ Σᵧ p(x,y) = 1. Marginal distributions are obtained by summing out the other variable: pₓ(x) = Σᵧ p(x,y). Covariance Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y] measures linear association, and the correlation coefficient ρ = Cov(X,Y) / (σₓ σᵧ) scales it to [−1, 1]. X and Y are independent if and only if p(x,y) = pₓ(x)·pᵧ(y) for all x, y.

rooms.question: X and Y have joint PMF given by: p(1,1)=0.1, p(1,2)=0.2, p(2,1)=0.3, p(2,2)=0.4. Find the marginal P(X=1).
steps: Sum over all values of y: p(1,1)+p(1,2).

rooms.question: Using the same joint PMF, are X and Y independent?
answers: No — the factorization fails for (X=1, Y=1).
Yes — the joint probabilities sum to 1.
Yes — because the marginals are well-defined.
No — because Cov(X,Y) cannot be zero.
rooms.explanation: Independence requires p(x,y) = pₓ(x)·pᵧ(y) for every (x,y). Here P(X=1)·P(Y=1) = 0.3×0.4 = 0.12 ≠ 0.1 = p(1,1), so X and Y are not independent.

rooms.question: X and Y have E[X] = 2, E[Y] = 3, E[XY] = 7. What is Cov(X, Y)?
If Cov(X, Y) = 4, Var(X) = 16, and Var(Y) = 4, what is the correlation coefficient ρ?
Order these statements about correlation ρ from weakest to strongest linear relationship:
rooms.explanation: The correlation coefficient ranges from −1 to 1. Closer to 0 means weaker linear association, and |ρ| = 1 means a perfect linear relationship. The order from weakest to strongest is ρ = 0, 0.3, 0.7, 1.

boss.name: The Correlation Phantom
boss.taunt: Your variables are entangled in me. You cannot separate what was never independent!

questions.question: If X and Y are independent, what must Cov(X, Y) equal?
questions.explanation: Independence implies E[XY] = E[X]E[Y], so Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y] = 0. Caution: the converse is false — zero covariance does not guarantee independence.

questions.question: For the joint PMF table with p(1,1)=0.1, p(1,2)=0.2, p(2,1)=0.3, p(2,2)=0.4, compute E[XY].
Which statement about the correlation coefficient ρ is always true?
answers: |\rho| \leq 1, with \rho = \pm1 iff Y = aX + b for some constants a,b
\rho = 0 implies X and Y are independent
\rho is only defined for normal random variables
questions.explanation: The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality guarantees |ρ| ≤ 1, with equality iff X and Y are in a perfect linear relationship. Zero correlation does not imply independence in general; causation is unrelated to correlation; and ρ is defined for any random variables with finite variance.
